text: The surgeon identifies the proximal and distal ends of the abnormal bowel segment. The mesentery is held perpendicular to the instrument to create a mesenteric window for bowel transection. A 2.5 mm linear stapler is used to divide the small bowel through the mesenteric window. The mesentery of the specimen is then divided using an electrosurgical device.

The surgeon places a traction suture at the anti-mesenteric side of the bowel to align the two portions before re-anastomosis. Hook electrocautery is used to create openings in the bowel lumen, taking care to avoid injury to the back wall of the bowel.

The surgeon uses a 60mm long, 2.5mm linear stapler to reestablish intestinal continuity, ensuring the stapler is fully placed within the bowel lumen and the bowel is evenly aligned. The stapled suture line is inspected for bleeding, which is controlled with interrupted sutures if present. A proximal stitch is then applied.

The surgeon closes the common enterotomy using a running stitch with absorbable suture, ensuring minimal tissue handling. A target suture is placed to aid dexterity, and the closure is performed in a single layer. Alternatively, a stapled closure is executed in a Heinecke-Mekulitz orientation, where the enterotomy is closed transversely.

The video shows the completed suture line alongside the staple line. A stay suture is placed at the end of the mesenteric defect, followed by a running suture to close the remainder of the defect, concluding at the previously placed stay suture.
